item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading this in now, you can definitely see some parallels with Westworld

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept and the ambiguity that comes with the description of how the entire ordering process works out. I would really like to see a tale of this, perhaps it would give us more depth to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know we arent supposed to reply to year old comments, but what if they suddenly mutate into something that makes that scp that much worse? you just cross tested every scp that they work on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the MTF name a reference to something that I'm not picking up on?

EDIT: Also I'm pretty sure the name should have initial capitals for every word, not just the first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. This is one of my new favourite skips. +1.

item.title: Ethical question
item.content:encoded:
If this existed, would it be unethical to use it?

The utilitarian in me says that no, it wouldn't be unethical to use it. The last remaining fragments of deontologist and/or virtue ethicist within me squeal and shudder at the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit. Finally!

But seriously, yellowstones sound uncomfortable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No clue. Maybe it's related to Samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's a Yellowstone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe Lempster, WY is near Yellowstone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now real life is disappointing. Oh well…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because it seems like something perfect with no downside that i would certainly use, and downvoted because it seems like something perfect with no downside that I would certainly use. Perfect is boring. Neutral Vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm quite on the fence with this one. The basic concept of a place that makes humans for you is not tremendously interesting to me, but a good number of the ways it has been explored I really liked. For example, I thought the catalogue samples were very good, and I liked the conversation with the call centre employee and the manager, especially the hint that the sellers might not be human themselves. I also liked the interview a lot.

There were a lot of bits I didn't like, though, which feel like they're a bit too clichéd (with the SCP-verse), such as all the things that just spontaneously appear and disappear (which comes across as a bit lazy, like you couldn't figure out how to make it work), and the conversation with Dr Gale, which seemed like 'let's make him extra creepy by making him talk weird and knowing things he shouldn't know'. It also seems extremely US-centric.

All of this together added up to an upvote, just. I think there's too much focus on the company and the factory, which may be the Foundation's greatest interest in-universe, but isn't really the interesting part to the reader - the interesting part is the humans. More about them and less about the company and I think it would stand better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe say 'in the direction of Wyoming'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's, erm, not unrelated to Dial-A-Llama, but it redeemed itself to me with the point where the man looks at the map of Wyoming and says it's wrong. That was very neatly subtle.

I'm also completely unsurprised that some people in the Foundation would look at using this a great source of D-class personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: LOLFoundation generally describes situations where the foundation acts like a bunch of juvenile, wasteful individuals with poor impulse control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I decided to drop the interview. It didn't really add much to the story as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The researcher notes at the end seem very out of place as well, and are a bit too LOLFoundation for my taste.

I'm sorry, I'm pretty new to the SCP foundation. What does "LOLfoundation" mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea, but some of the writing is iffy in some places and I feel like you're adding a lot of unnecessary detail with the interviews..

The researcher notes at the end seem very out of place as well, and are a bit too LOLFoundation for my taste.

I love it overall, though. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see the justification for terminating a particularly threatening instance of SCP-1532-a, but, like what Mechaelite said, the two interviewed instances didn't do anything to necessitate termination. They should have been handled in some other fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Tests of these catalogues show that they not anomalous " are not*
Otherwise it's a pretty solid article right now, so +1. Enjoyable piece.
Really dig these Dial-A-Llamma type SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One bit of advice that'll help to take you far around here:
If you don't know, don't assume. We have a great wide Internet, and it should be minimal trouble to find what you need.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem! This is a great idea, really loving it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, no worries. The important thing is that it was fixed. Sorry I couldn't take care of it…stupid computer >:(

Good job on the scip though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really dislike interview 1. Otherwise, this is pretty good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is sound and most of your writing is good, but the dialogue is a little stilted in places and some of your description gets a bit muddy toward the end. Also, I'll agree this is a little too similar to "Dial a Llama" for peoples, without matching the opportunity for weirdness.

I'm left with "eh…" so no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, hell, a factory that creates humans for humans.

Alright I like this one, but I can't stop thinking that if we're unable to locate the factory where they are being made, we may have a problem.

Also terminating every SCP-1532-a instance is like if the foundation had just become the GOC. If we have captured one, I'd suggest ussing it as a D until the month ends and we have to terminate it or until an SCP kills it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Noncoding DNA" could really be narrowed down; you could, for example, write "a specific non-coding sequence on Chromosome 19 that is not found anywhere else in nature."

Yeah, I actually don't know anything about genetics. If "a specific non-coding sequence on Chromosome 19" is a real genetic term, then I'll use it. I kept it non-specific because I didn't want to make a big error.

Two million copies? Nope. Foundation wouldn't be able to get them all or remove the memories of people who did. If 1% made an order, that's 20,000 humans that the Foundation has to round up before the police departments start getting conflicting information. I can't imagine the Foundation being able to deal with this containment breach… Should be Keter, if they have no way of actually stopping it from spamming two million mailboxes with its shit.

If the choice is either tone it done from two million copies, or up the Object class to Keter, I'll tone it down. It's not meant to be an actual threat, because that would change the fringe horror into normal horror, and that isn't what I want.
Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you have two uses of the word "currant" … The word you should be using is "current" with an "e".

Yeah, I'm pretty dyslexic and I mix up my words a lot. But I fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good things:

  1. Straightforward concept that's easy to grasp.
  2. Good writing in the catalog excerpts.
  3. Good dialogue throughout.
  4. Fridge horror at the whole thing.

Bad things:

  1. If there's a media campaign about how Dr. Gale's Human Emporium is a fraud, why haven't I heard of it? And why can't I find out about it on Google? >.>
  2. "Noncoding DNA" could really be narrowed down; you could, for example, write "a specific non-coding sequence on Chromosome 19 that is not found anywhere else in nature."
  3. Two million copies? Nope. Foundation wouldn't be able to get them all or remove the memories of people who did. If 1% made an order, that's 20,000 humans that the Foundation has to round up before the police departments start getting conflicting information. I can't imagine the Foundation being able to deal with this containment breach.
  4. The four notes at the end don't add anything, really. The last log makes a perfectly good ending for the article.
  5. Should be Keter, if they have no way of actually stopping it from spamming two million mailboxes with its shit.

Fix the last things and you have my upvote. Otherwise, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right. Wikidot you win. You've kicked me off editor every time for the last twenty minutes. Because either Wikidot or my computer are royally screwing up and not letting me edit, you have two uses of the word "currant" in Addendum 2 and also in your final collapsible, both times when talking about Lempster and its not being on maps. The word you should be using is "current" with an "e". I give up, I would fix it for you, but something is not working on my end. Sorry.

Now, onto the article. Overall, I like it. It's well thought out, it is creepy in the fridge logic (if something is making humans like toys and delivering them, then the creators are likely not human and humans are likely not the dominant lifeform), and it's interesting, which are all things I look for in a good SCP. Not everything has to present mind you, but they help quite a lot in securing my upvote. It is…oddly formatted in some of your style choices, but that's what the "no canon" thing is great for. So you have my upvote good sir or madam. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'll use that sentence verbatim. Thanks for the suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe you could say something like,

The van spontaneously appears eight kilometers away from its destination, drops off an instance of SCP-1532-A, and then disappears after traveling another eight kilometers. All attempts to follow or track the van have failed.

Just a suggestion though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That kinda sounds like the van travels eight kilometers to the house and then disappears.

It's meant to mean that the van starts at a point eight kilometers away from the house, drives to the house and drops off the person, then drives eight kilometers away before disappearing. I could change the wording if you can think of a better way to explain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried that in the first draft; with SCP-1532 representing the phenomena of -a's and -b's spontaneously appearing, but the whole article came out really stilted and forced. But I added some more notes at the end that explore the possibility that the facility doesn't exist at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One step closer to Kronos Heaven! I love the pamplet examples, but I saw something strange.

the van will only travel approximately eight kilometers to or from its destination before spontaneously disappearing.

That kinda sounds like the van travels eight kilometers to the house and then disappears.
Other than that, love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed all of the "SPC"s typos I could find, but if there are any others I missed, feel free to fix them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think calling the facility the SCP is a mistake. Rather than starting us off with what we don't know, start us off with what we do know: that flyers advertising build-your-own-human services will show up randomly, and filling them out causes a -a to show up in a van later. Then throw in a note that we have no idea where these things are made because the vans disappear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but I agree with Accelerando that this needs cleaned up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a lot of typos and SPC's. This reminds me of Dial-A-Llama too much for me to upvote, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, thanks for the catch. And xièxie (Chinese for "thank you") for broadening my linguistic knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It means "For you" in Italian. I always say that when I fix something. Your title was "Scp 1532" instead of "SCP-1532".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the title per te.

I don't know what "per te" means, but thanks for fixing… whatever it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the title per te.
